answer:
The power of \( m(t) = n(t) \cos(\omega_0 t + \theta) \) is given by \( E[m^2(t)] = E[n^2(t)] \, E[\cos^2(\omega_0 t + \theta)] \).  
Since \( \theta \) is uniformly distributed over \( [-\pi, \pi] \), the time average of \( \cos^2 \) is \( 1/2 \), and independence gives \( \sigma^2 \cdot (1/2) \).  
Thus the power dissipated is \( \frac{1}{2} \sigma^2 \), corresponding to option I.

\boxed{I}
